title: New Beginning
html:
The cake had 16 candles and had cost most of what he’d earned last week. He’d bought it at the stall run by the widow two villages over, carried it back in both hands, set it on the table. The birthday song was playing from his phone propped against the wall. The screen light was the only light in the room.

He hadn’t lit the candles yet.

Lucas sat across from it and thought about his parents. Memories of their faces had started going blurry at the edges a few years ago. Now when he tried to picture them he got shape and impression more than detail — his mother’s laugh, his father’s hands. He’d stopped forcing it. Forcing it made the blurry parts worse.

He lit the candles. Watched them for a moment.

“Mom, Dad. Sixteen.” He exhaled and blew them out.

He was cutting the first slice when the static started.

“zzz…z…zz.”

He stopped and pressed two fingers to his jaw, working his ear the way he did when water got in from diving. The static faded. He waited. Nothing came after it.

‘Auditory hallucination,’ he decided. He’d read something once about exhaustion producing that. He’d worked six of the last seven days. That was probably what this was.

---

【HUMAN OF EARTH. INITIATING EARTH MUTATION PROGRAM: PHASE 1.】

---

He picked up the cake and ate.

Screaming outside woke him near midnight — a woman’s voice, high and short, cut off mid-sound. He pulled the blanket over his head and waited for the village to deal with it. The screaming didn’t come back. He was asleep again within a few minutes.

***

January 21, 2029

He woke to light coming through the wall planks and the sound of water. He lay still and checked his body the way he did every morning: shoulders stiff but usable, hands okay, right knee sore from the construction site two days ago but he could walk on it. Good enough for fishing.

He swung off the cot and opened the front door.

The dock was there. The boat wasn’t.

He stood in the doorway and looked at the water where his boat should have been. He scanned the lake surface left to right — no hull, no rope trailing. The tie-off post had a frayed end still knotted around it. Someone had cut the rope, which meant they’d come with a knife and knew what they were taking.

‘Stolen.’ He’d had the boat since he was twelve, bought with three months of saved labor. It was old and slow but it worked.

He closed the front door and went out through the back instead, the way that connected to solid ground. He picked up the fishing spear he kept leaning against the rear wall. Bank fishing was slower than boat fishing but he could still eat.

He was halfway down the bank when he saw the fish.

It was lying in the shallows where the reeds thinned, about 30 cm long. He’d fished this lake for six years and knew most of what lived in it. This one he didn’t recognize — the body shape was slightly off, the fins placed wrong, the coloring darker than anything he’d seen here. It was alive, moving through the shallows in slow turns that didn’t look right, not the normal glide of something feeding.

He came closer with the spear low.

Slash! He drove the point into the water and pinned it to the lakebed. The fish thrashed once and went still. Green blood spread through the water around the tip.

He lifted the spear and looked at it. ‘Green blood.’ He’d gutted hundreds of fish on this bank. Red, always. Sometimes dark, sometimes bright, but always red.

He carried it up and cleaned it on the flat rock he used for that. The flesh inside looked ordinary — pale, slightly darker near the spine. He couldn’t find anything wrong with it beyond the blood. He built the fire, fried it in the pan he kept by the back door, and ate it sitting on the bank while the smoke drifted sideways.

It tasted better than any fish he’d caught in a while. He scraped the pan clean with the flat of the chopstick.

---

【Mutated Fish Species Detected. Commencing System Profile.】

【Opening System.】

---

A large panel appeared in his field of vision. He blinked. It stayed.

---

Name: Lucas Tharla

Age: 16

Race: Human

Gender: Male

Class: None

Subclass: None

Subclass: None

Subclass: None

Level: 0 (0/10)

Stats:

Agi: 10 (Average Adult Human: 20)

Str: 20 (Average Adult Human: 20)

Int: 25 (Average Adult Human: 20)

Vit: 25 (Average Adult Human: 20)

Unallocated Stat Points: 0

Unallocated Skill Points: 0

---

He read it twice.

‘Agi is low.’ He’d always assumed he was at least average on speed — he could outrun most of the other fishing boys when they raced for the first catch of the morning. Ten was apparently not average. Something to account for.

The panel looked like a screen from one of the games on his phone except it was floating in the air in front of him and he could see the lake through it. He passed his hand through it. Nothing. He looked away and it disappeared. He thought ‘System’ and it came back.

He set the pan down. ‘So that works.’

He looked at the lake.

There were more of the wrong-shaped fish in the shallows now — he counted ten, spread across maybe 5 meters of bank. They weren’t schooling, no coordinated direction, just occupying the same water. He watched them while he rinsed the spear.

Two of them moved toward the same patch of reeds. One was about 35 cm, the other closer to 40. They circled once, and then the larger one lunged — Snap! He heard it from the bank — and caught the smaller one by the midsection and held it. He expected it to let go. It didn’t. It ate the smaller fish in pieces, and while he watched, it grew. Not slowly. Inside 20 seconds it was visibly larger, the body wider, the fins filling out.

‘They eat each other and get bigger.’ He turned this over once. ‘That means they’ll keep getting bigger if nothing stops them.’

He picked up the spear and waded in.

The fish scattered toward deeper water. The large one held its position. He’d seen fish do this before — the ones that had grown big enough to stop running got slow and stayed put, and that was usually the moment they got caught. He moved toward it with the spear low and to the side, not overhead where the shadow would land first.

Slash! He drove it in and caught the fish through the side, not clean but enough. It thrashed hard and nearly pulled the spear sideways before going still. He dragged it back to the bank and measured it against his forearm. About 40 cm, maybe a little more.

Green blood in the water behind him.

---

【Level 2 Mutated Fish Killed. Awarding 10 EXP.】

【Leveling EXP Reached: 10/10.】

【Level Up: Level 0 → Level 1.】

【Rewards: 2 Stat Points, 1 Skill Point.】

---

He thought ‘System.’

---

Name: Lucas Tharla

Age: 16

Race: Human

Gender: Male

Class: None

Subclass: None

Subclass: None

Subclass: None

Level: 1 (0/50)

Stats:

Agi: 10 (Average Adult Human: 20)

Str: 20 (Average Adult Human: 20)

Int: 25 (Average Adult Human: 20)

Vit: 25 (Average Adult Human: 20)

Unallocated Stat Points: 2

Unallocated Skill Points: 1

---

‘Two points to put somewhere. One skill point.’ He closed the panel and cleaned the second fish. He was still hungry — he’d had one slice of cake the night before and nothing else. He built the fire back up and ate the second fish while it was still hot, going through it down to the bones.

He’d leave the stat points for now. Putting them somewhere before he understood what the numbers meant in practice was just waste, and he didn’t have the margin for waste.

He rinsed the pan in the lake, leaned the spear against the dock post, and went inside to get the tin from under the floorboard.

He’d been out of rice for two days, rationing the last of it and then running out anyway. He had enough money for a few kilos. The market was twenty minutes on foot by the back path, up through the tree line and along the main track into the village. He’d walked it hundreds of times.

He went out through the back and took the path up the slope.

He walked for ten minutes.

He hadn’t seen anyone.

Lucas slowed to a stop in the middle of the track. From here he could see the rooftops of the closest houses at the edge of the village. He listened. No voices, no dogs barking at him through the fence, no sounds from the market stalls. The corner house where the dogs lived was quiet.

He thought about the screaming the night before. The way it had cut off.

He stood on the path and looked at the village.
